title: Streams, Super Streams, and Workload Choice · Language Lens: Queue Stream Semantics
summary: A Go 1.26 service team must select queue, stream, or super stream from retention, replay, throughput, partition, client, and operations evidence; reviewers reject hidden goroutine ownership, unbounded work, stale channel state, and claims that stop at a nil client error. The learner predicts values, ownership transitions, and stop paths before seeing a model. Planned artifact: Add and verify the queue stream semantics increment in a queue-versus-stream Go client decision record. Learners progre…
objectives: Compare destructive queue consumption with retained stream offsets, replay, retention, protocol clients, and consumer responsibility; in Go, retain ownership, stop-path, and changed-case evidence.
Measure throughput, tail latency, skew, recovery, and correctness when selecting queue count, consumer concurrency, and order scope; in Go, retain ownership, stop-path, and changed-case evidence.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: RabbitMQ and Go Predict Queue Stream Semantics · Theory Queue Stream Semantics
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go messaging team handles case 6027 during outbox relay recovery after a database commit. Build deterministic Go evidence to select queue, stream, or super stream from retention, replay, throughput, and partition evidence; name the owner and stop path for every goroutine and notification channel; then make one state notification stale after recovery generation changes. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. amqp091-go, AMQP sockets, RabbitMQ, TLS, partitions, restarts, Docker, databases, race and leak checks, load, upgrades, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.
